volumeTitle: The Merchant. The Braggart Soldier. The Ghost. The Persian
label: The Braggart Soldier (Section 4.1.960 - Section 4.2.1015)

translation.label: Scaife English translation
translation.citation: Miles Gloriosus, or The Braggart Captain

sections.label: Section 4.1.960
sections.original: eius nunc mi ánulum ad te ancilla porro ut deferrem dedit .
sections.translation: Her maid just now gave me the ring that I might then give it to you .

sections.label: Section 4.1.961
sections.original: Quid ea ? ingenuan an festuca facta e serva liberast ?
sections.translation: What ? Is she free born or a freed woman , made free from a slave by the Praetor ’ s rod [*] ?

sections.label: Section 4.1.962
sections.original: Vah , egone ut ad te ab libertina esse auderem internuntius ,
sections.translation: Pshaw ! Should I presume to be the bearer of a message to you from a person once a slave , who cannot sufficiently answer the demands of the free women who are longing for you ?

sections.label: Section 4.1.964_965
sections.original: Nuptan est an vidua ?
sections.translation: Is she wife , or is she widow ?

sections.label: Section 4.1.964_965b
sections.original: Et nupta et vidua .
sections.translation: She is both wife and widow .

sections.label: Section 4.1.964_965c
sections.original: Quo pacto potis
sections.translation: In what way is it possible for the same woman to be a wife and a widow ?

sections.label: Section 4.1.966b
sections.original: Quia adulescens nuptast cum sene .
sections.translation: Because she is a young woman married to an old man .

sections.label: Section 4.1.967
sections.original: Euge .
sections.translation: That ’ s good .

sections.label: Section 4.1.967b
sections.original: Lepida et liberali formast .
sections.translation: She is of genteel and charming person .

sections.label: Section 4.1.967c
sections.original: Cave mendacium .
sections.translation: Beware of misrepresenting .

sections.label: Section 4.1.968
sections.original: Ad tuam formam illa una dignast .
sections.translation: It is alone worthy to be compared with your own charms .

sections.label: Section 4.1.968b
sections.original: Hercle pulchram praedicas .
sections.translation: By my faith , you make her out to be a beauty . But who is she ?

sections.label: Section 4.1.969b
sections.original: Senis huius uxor Periplectomeni ex proxumo ;
sections.translation: The wife of that old gentleman , Periplecomenus , next door .

sections.label: Section 4.1.970
sections.original: ea demoritur te atque ab illo cupit abire : odit senem .
sections.translation: She is dying for you , and wishes to leave him ; she hates the old fellow . Now she has begged me to entreat and beseech you that you will give her your support and assistance .

sections.label: Section 4.1.972b
sections.original: Cupio hercle equidem , si illa volt .
sections.translation: I ’ faith , I ’ m ready for my part if she desires it .

sections.label: Section 4.1.973
sections.original: Quae cupit ?
sections.translation: Doesn ’ t she long for it ?

sections.label: Section 4.1.973b
sections.original: Quid illá faciemus concubina , quae domist ?
sections.translation: What shall we do with that mistress of mine , who is at my house ?

sections.label: Section 4.1.974
sections.original: Quin tu illam iube abs te abire quo lubet : sicut soror
sections.translation: Why , do you bid her to be gone about her business , wherever she chooses ; as her

sections.label: Section 4.1.975
sections.original: eius huc gémina venit Ephesum et mater , accersuntque eam .
sections.translation: twin-sister has come here to Ephesus , and her mother , and they are come to fetch her .

sections.label: Section 4.1.976
sections.original: Eho tu , an venit Ephesum mater eius ?
sections.translation: Ha ! what ’ s that you say ? Has her mother come to Ephesus ?

sections.label: Section 4.1.976b
sections.original: Aiunt qui sciunt .
sections.translation: Those say so who know it .

sections.label: Section 4.1.977
sections.original: Hercle occasionem lepidam , ut mulierem excludam foras .
sections.translation: I ’ faith , a charming opportunity for me to turn the wench out of doors .

sections.label: Section 4.1.978
sections.original: Immo vin tu lepide facere ?
sections.translation: Aye , but do you wish to do the thing handsomely ?

sections.label: Section 4.1.978b
sections.original: Loquere et consilium cedo .
sections.translation: Speak out , and give me your advice .

sections.label: Section 4.1.979
sections.original: Vin tu illam actutum amovere , a te ut abeat per gratiam ?
sections.translation: Do you wish to pack her off forthwith , that she may quit you with a good grace ?

sections.label: Section 4.1.980
sections.original: Cupio .
sections.translation: I do so wish .

sections.label: Section 4.1.980b
sections.original: Tum te hoc facere oportet . tibi divitiarum adfatimst :
sections.translation: Then this is the thing you must do . You have a superabundance of wealth ; bid the woman to keep as a present for herself the gold and trinkets which you have supplied her with , and to take herself off from your house wherever she likes .

sections.label: Section 4.1.983
sections.original: Placet ut dicis ; sed ne et istam amittam et haec mutet fidem
sections.translation: It pleases me what you say ; but yet , only think , if I should lose her , and the other change her mind ?

sections.label: Section 4.1.984b
sections.original: Vah delicatus esdelicatu's , quae te tamquam oculos amet .
sections.translation: Pshaw ! you ’ re over nice ; a lady , that loves you as her own eyes .

sections.label: Section 4.1.985
sections.original: Venus me amat .
sections.translation: Venus befriends me .

sections.label: Section 4.1.985b
sections.original: St tace , aperitur foris , concede huc clanculum .
sections.translation: Hist ! hush ! the door is opening ; come this way a little out of sight . ( MILPHIDIPPA comes out of the house of PERIPLECOMENUS .) This is her fly-boat — her go-between , that ’ s coming out there .

sections.label: Section 4.1.987b
sections.original: Quae haec celox ?
sections.translation: How so — fly-boat ?

sections.label: Section 4.1.987c
sections.original: Ancillula illiust , quae hinc egreditur foras .
sections.translation: This is her maid that is coming out of the house , she that brought that ring which I delivered to you .

sections.label: Section 4.1.988b
sections.original: Edepol haec quidem
sections.translation: I ’ faith , she too is a prettyish wench .

sections.label: Section 4.1.989b
sections.original: Pithecium haec est prae illa et spinturnicium .
sections.translation: This one is a little monkey and an owl [*] in comparison with the other .

sections.label: Section 4.1.990
sections.original: viden tu illam oculis venaturam facere atque aucupium auribus ?
sections.translation: Do you see how she hunts around with her eyes , and goes fowling about with her ears .

sections.label: Section 4.2.991
sections.original: Iam est ante aedis circus ubi sunt ludi faciundi mihi .
sections.translation: My Circus , then [*] , is before the house , where my sports are to take place . I ’ ll make pretence , as though I didn ’ t see them , or knew as yet that they are here .

sections.label: Section 4.2.993
sections.original: Tace , subauscultemus ecquid de me fiat mentio .
sections.translation: Hush ! let ’ s quietly listen , whether any mention is made of me .

sections.label: Section 4.2.994
sections.original: Numquis nam hic própe adest qui rem alienam potius curet quam suam ,
sections.translation: Is there no one near at hand here , to attend to another ’ s business rather than his own ?—

sections.label: Section 4.2.995
sections.original: qui aucupet me quid agam , qui de vesperi vivat suo ?
sections.translation: to prowl after [*] me to see what I ’ m about ? No one who is feeding this evening [*] at his own expense ? I dread such men as these , lest they should now come in the way , or prove an hindrance somehow , should my mistress privately pass from her house this way , who is so enamoured of his person , who so dotes upon this very charming man with his exceeding beauty — the Captain Pyrgopolinices

sections.label: Section 4.2.999b
sections.original: Satin haec quoque me deperit ?
sections.translation: And doesn ’ t she dote upon me , too ? She is praising my beauty .

sections.label: Section 4.2.1000
sections.original: meam laudat speciem . edepol huius sermo haud cinerem quaeritat .
sections.translation: I ’ faith , her language stands in need of no ashes [*] . [*]

sections.label: Section 4.2.1001
sections.original: Quo argumento ?
sections.translation: For what reason ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.1001b
sections.original: Quia enim loquitur laute et minime sordide .
sections.translation: Why , because her language is clean spoken and far from slovenly .

sections.label: Section 4.2.1002
sections.original: Quippini ? istaec de te loquitur : nihil attrectat sordidi .
sections.translation: Whatever she says about yourself , she handles it in no slovenly way .

sections.label: Section 4.2.1003
sections.original: Tum autem illa ipsa est nimium lepida nimisque nitida femina .
sections.translation: And , then , besides , she herself is a very pretty and a very dainty wench .

sections.label: Section 4.2.1004
sections.original: hercle vero iam adlubescit primulum , Palaestrio .
sections.translation: Troth , indeed , she has made an impression already , Palaestrio , at first sight .

sections.label: Section 4.2.1005
sections.original: Priusne quam illam oculis tuis videas ?
sections.translation: What ! before you have seen the other with your eyes ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.1005b
sections.original: Video id quod credo tibi .
sections.translation: What I see , in that I have faith for myself ; for this mackerel [*] , in the absence of the mullet , compels me to be in love with her .

sections.label: Section 4.2.1006b
sections.original: Hercle hanc quidem
sections.translation: I ’ faith , you really mustn ’ t be falling in love with her , she ’ s engaged to me . If the other weds you to-day , forthwith I shall take this one for my wife .

sections.label: Section 4.2.1008b
sections.original: Quíd ergo hanc dubitas conloqui ?
sections.translation: Why , then , do you delay to accost her ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.1009
sections.original: Sequere hac me ergo .
sections.translation: Follow me this way , then .

sections.label: Section 4.2.1009b
sections.original: Pedisequos tibi sum .
sections.translation: I am your lackey at your heels [*] .

sections.label: Section 4.2.1009c
sections.original: Vtinam , cuius causa foras
sections.translation: I wish that I had an opportunity of meeting him on account of whom I came here out of doors .

sections.label: Section 4.2.1011
sections.original: Erit , ét tibi exoptatum óptinget , bonum habe ánimum , ne formída ;
sections.translation: It shall be so , and you shall have what you so greatly wish ; be of good courage , don ’ t fear ; there is a certain person who knows where that is which you are seeking .

sections.label: Section 4.2.1012b
sections.original: Quem ego hic audívi ?
sections.translation: Who ’ s that I hear at hand ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.1013
sections.original: Sociúm tuorum concíliorum et partícipem consiliorum .
sections.translation: The sharer of your plans and the partaker of your secrets .

sections.label: Section 4.2.1014
sections.original: Tum pól ego id quod celo haú celo .
sections.translation: I ’ faith , then , what I do conceal I don ’ t conceal .

sections.label: Section 4.2.1014b
sections.original: Immo et célas et non célas .
sections.translation: Aye , but still you don ’ t conceal it this way .

sections.label: Section 4.2.1015
sections.original: Quo argúmento ?
sections.translation: How so ?
